content:
The idea that models should predict **new observations**, not merely refit old ones, is central to **statistics** and **machine learning** from the start. **Gauss** and **Legendre** fit linear models on samples and applied them to fresh measurements — the earliest notion of generalizing beyond the fitting set.

**Vapnik** and **Chervonenkis** (1970s) formalized **generalization theory** with VC dimension and uniform convergence — bounding how well empirical risk on a finite sample predicts true risk on unseen data.

**Occam's razor** and **regularization** traditions (Tikhonov, ridge regression) explicitly traded training fit for better out-of-sample prediction — recognizing that minimizing training error alone does not guarantee generalization.

The **1990s** brought **bias–variance decomposition** (Geman et al.) and practical **cross-validation** workflows, giving practitioners vocabulary and procedures to estimate generalization before deployment.

**Deep learning's** resurgence (2010s) revived debates on **implicit regularization** and **double descent**, but the operational criterion unchanged: models are judged on **held-out** and **live** performance, not training loss alone.

Modern **MLOps** treats generalization monitoring — offline test metrics, drift detection, periodic revalidation — as a continuous lifecycle concern, not a one-time offline calculation.

timeline.label: Classical prediction (1800s–1900s)
timeline.description: Parametric models fit on samples and applied to new observations — generalization as predictive inference beyond the fitting set.

timeline.label: VC theory (1970s)
timeline.description: Vapnik–Chervonenkis framework links model complexity to generalization bounds on unseen data.

timeline.label: Bias–variance (1990s)
timeline.description: Formal decomposition explains overfitting as low training error with poor generalization to test data.

timeline.label: Cross-validation adoption (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: k-fold and hold-out procedures become standard for estimating generalization during model selection.

timeline.label: MLOps & drift monitoring (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Production systems track whether live data still matches training assumptions — generalization as an ongoing operational metric.
